refashioned:
In all friendships between unlike people, as we have said, it is proportional exchange that equalizes the parties and preserves the friendship. In political or transactional friendship, for instance, the shoemaker receives payment for his shoes in proportion to their worth, and the weaver and every other craftsman does the same. Here money has been established as a common measure, and so everything is referred to it and measured by it.

But in the friendship of lovers, sometimes the lover complains that his intense love is not returned in kind -- though perhaps there is nothing lovable about him -- while often the beloved complains that the lover, who once promised everything, now delivers nothing. Such things happen when the lover loves the beloved for the sake of pleasure, while the beloved loves the lover for the sake of utility, and neither actually possesses what the other expected. When these are the grounds of the friendship, it dissolves once those qualities disappear, since neither loved the other person as such, but only the qualities the other had -- and those were not enduring. That is why such friendships are also transient. But the love grounded in character, as we have said, endures because it depends on what each person truly is.

Differences arise when people receive something other than what they desired, for getting something other than what we aimed at is like getting nothing at all. Consider the story of the man who made promises to a lyre-player, pledging him greater rewards the better he sang; but in the morning, when the player demanded fulfillment of the promises, the man said he had already given pleasure in return for pleasure. If that had been what both wanted, all would have been well. But if one wanted enjoyment while the other wanted profit, and only one got what he wanted, the terms of the relationship have not been properly fulfilled. For what each person actually wants is what he attends to, and it is for the sake of that that he will give what he has.
elegant: In friendships between unlike people, proportional exchange preserves the relationship. Money serves as a common measure in transactional friendships, but in romantic and personal relationships, conflicts arise when the parties seek different things -- one seeking pleasure, the other utility. When neither loves the other person as such but only certain qualities, the friendship dissolves once those qualities fade. Only friendship grounded in character endures, because it depends on who each person truly is. When people receive something other than what they desired, it is as if they received nothing at all, for each person gives only for the sake of what he himself wants in return.
summary: Friendships between unlike people survive through proportional exchange. When lovers or friends seek different things from the relationship -- one wanting pleasure, the other utility -- conflict arises and the friendship dissolves once those qualities fade. Only character-based friendship endures because it is not dependent on transient qualities.
distilled: Friendships between dissimilar people endure only when there is proportional exchange -- and only character-based friendship is truly stable, since it does not depend on fleeting qualities.
notes: Continues the analysis of unequal friendships from Book VIII.8-14. -- The lyre-player anecdote is otherwise unattested and may be a common fable used in philosophical schools. -- The distinction between loving a person vs. loving their qualities is central to Aristotle's friendship typology (VIII.3-4). -- 'Political friendship' (politike philia) refers to civic/commercial relationships based on exchange, not modern political friendship. -- The principle that proportional equality preserves friendship draws on the mathematical proportion theory from Book V on justice.

refashioned:
But who should determine the worth of the service -- the one who makes the sacrifice or the one who receives the benefit? At any rate, the other party seems to leave it to the receiver. This is what they say Protagoras used to do: whenever he taught any subject, he would tell the student to assess how much the knowledge was worth to him, and then accepted whatever amount the student named.

But in such matters, some people prefer the principle "let a man have his fixed reward agreed upon in advance." Those who take their payment first and then fail to deliver what they promised -- because their promises were extravagant -- naturally become objects of complaint, for they do not fulfill their agreement. The sophists are perhaps forced to operate this way, because no one would pay money for what they actually know. When people fail to deliver what they have been paid for, they naturally invite complaint.
elegant: Who should determine the value of a service -- the giver or the receiver? Protagoras let his students set the price of his teaching. Some prefer fixed fees agreed in advance. Those who take payment first but fail to deliver what they promised rightly invite complaint. The sophists must rely on advance payment because no one would pay for their knowledge after receiving it.
summary: The question of who should set the value of a service is difficult. Protagoras let students assess the worth of his teaching themselves. Those who demand payment upfront but fail to deliver -- as the sophists often must -- rightly face complaints.
distilled: The value of a service should ideally be set by the receiver, but those who take payment before delivering must fulfill what they promised or face justified complaint.
notes: Protagoras of Abdera (c. 490-420 BC): the most famous of the sophists, known for the doctrine 'man is the measure of all things'; Plato's dialogue Protagoras depicts his approach to teaching. -- Aristotle's critique of the sophists here is gentler than in Sophistical Refutations, where he attacks their methods more directly. -- The 'fixed reward' saying may echo Hesiod's Works and Days on fair dealing. -- The question of pricing knowledge foreshadows debates about commodifying education that persist to this day.

refashioned:
But where there is no contract for services, those who give something for the sake of the other person cannot, as we have said, be justly complained of -- for that is the very nature of virtue-based friendship -- and the return to them must be made on the basis of their intention, for it is intention that is the defining feature of both a friend and a virtuous person.

This seems to be the right approach, too, toward those with whom one has studied philosophy: their worth cannot be measured in money, and no amount of honor could balance what they have given. Yet it is perhaps enough, as with the gods and one's parents, to give them what one can.
elegant: In virtue-based friendships with no service contract, there can be no legitimate complaint, since the giver acts from good intention. Returns should match the giver's purpose, not a market price. Those who teach us philosophy, like gods and parents, deserve more than we can ever repay -- yet giving what we can is enough.
summary: In friendships based on virtue with no explicit agreement, the giver cannot be rightfully complained about. The return should match the giver's intention. Teachers of philosophy, like gods and parents, deserve more than can ever be repaid, but giving what one can suffices.
distilled: Virtue-based friendship requires no contract -- returns should match the giver's intention, and toward those who have given us wisdom, as toward gods and parents, we can only give what we are able.
notes: The comparison of philosophy teachers with gods and parents reflects the deep reverence for the teacher-student relationship in Greek culture, also seen in Plato's Republic and Laws. -- 'Purpose' (prohairesis) is Aristotle's key ethical concept, central to his account of virtue in Books II-III; here it defines the essential feature of friendship. -- The idea that some debts can never be fully repaid connects to the discussion of gratitude and honor in Book IV.

refashioned: If the gift was not of this sort -- that is, if it was made with an expectation of return -- then ideally the return should be one that seems fair to both parties. But if that cannot be achieved, it would seem not only necessary but also just for the person who receives the first service to determine the repayment. For if the beneficiary returns the equivalent of the advantage he received, or the price he would have paid for that pleasure, he will have received what is fair from the other person.
elegant: When a gift is given with expectation of return, the repayment should ideally satisfy both parties. If agreement cannot be reached, the recipient of the benefit should set the terms, returning the equivalent of what the service was worth to him.
summary: When a gift is made with an expectation of return and the parties cannot agree on fair repayment, the person who received the benefit should determine the value, returning the equivalent of the advantage gained.
distilled: When return is expected for a gift, the recipient should set the repayment at the value the benefit was actually worth to him.
notes: This passage develops the principle of corrective justice from Book V.4, applying it to informal exchanges between friends. -- The idea that the beneficiary determines repayment follows from the asymmetry of valuation discussed in the next passage (9.5).

refashioned:
We see this happening with goods put up for sale too. In some places, there are even laws providing that no legal actions can arise from voluntary contracts, on the assumption that one should settle with a person to whom one extended credit in the same spirit as when one originally made the bargain. The law holds that it is more just for the person who received credit to set the terms than for the person who extended it.

For most things are not valued the same way by those who already have them and those who want them: each party overvalues what is its own and what it is offering. Yet the return is made on the terms set by the receiver. But the receiver should assess the thing not at what it seems worth once he already has it, but at what he valued it before he had it.
elegant: Some laws prohibit legal claims from voluntary contracts, holding that disputes should be settled in the spirit of the original agreement. The receiver rather than the giver should set the terms of repayment. People overvalue what they have and what they offer, so the receiver must assess the benefit not at its value after receipt, but at what he judged it worth beforehand.
summary: In voluntary exchanges, the receiver should set the terms of repayment. Since people tend to overvalue what they already possess, the fair assessment is what the receiver judged the benefit to be worth before receiving it, not after.
distilled: Fair repayment requires the receiver to assess a benefit at the value he placed on it before receiving it, not after -- since possession distorts judgment.
notes: The principle that possession distorts valuation anticipates what behavioral economists now call the 'endowment effect.' -- The reference to laws governing voluntary contracts may reflect Athenian commercial law or practices in other Greek cities. -- This passage applies principles from Book V's discussion of justice in exchange (V.5) to the context of friendship.

refashioned: A further problem is raised by questions like these: Should one always defer to one's father and obey him in all things, or should one trust a doctor when ill, and elect a man of military skill when choosing a general? Similarly, should one do a favor for a friend in preference to a good man, and should one show gratitude to a benefactor rather than assist a friend, when one cannot do both?
elegant: Difficult questions arise about competing obligations: Must one always obey one's father, or trust a doctor when ill and a military expert when choosing a general? Should one favor a friend over a good man, or repay a benefactor rather than help a friend, when both cannot be done?
summary: Aristotle raises the problem of conflicting obligations: whether to always obey one's father, defer to expertise, or prioritize friends over benefactors when one cannot satisfy both duties simultaneously.
distilled: When obligations to parents, friends, benefactors, and experts conflict, one faces a genuine ethical dilemma about whom to prioritize.
notes: This aporetic passage sets up the discussion of competing claims that runs through IX.2. -- The tension between filial duty and other obligations is a recurring theme in Greek ethics; compare Socrates' argument in the Crito about obeying one's parents and the city. -- The examples (doctor, general) connect to Aristotle's frequent analogy between practical expertise and ethical judgment.

refashioned:
All such questions are difficult to decide with precision, are they not? For they admit of many variations in both the magnitude and the nobility or necessity of the service. But it is clear enough that we should not give the preference in all things to the same person, and that we must generally repay benefits received rather than do favors for friends -- just as we must repay a loan to a creditor before making a loan to a friend.

But perhaps even this is not always true. For example, should a man who has been ransomed from brigands ransom his rescuer in return, whoever that person may be -- or pay him back if the rescuer has not himself been captured but simply requests repayment -- or should he instead ransom his own father? It would seem that he should ransom his father in preference even to himself.

As we have said, then, generally the debt should be paid. But if the alternative gift is exceedingly noble or exceedingly necessary, one should defer to those considerations. For sometimes it is not even fair to return the equivalent of what one has received, as when one man has done a service to someone he knows to be virtuous, while the other must make a return to someone he believes to be wicked. Indeed, sometimes one should not even lend in return to someone who has lent to oneself, for the original lender lent to a good man expecting repayment, while the other has no hope of recovering from someone believed to be dishonest. If the facts truly are as described, the demand is not fair; and even if they are not, but people believe them to be so, those who refuse would not be thought to be acting strangely.

As we have often pointed out, then, discussions about feelings and actions can only be as precise as their subject matter allows.
elegant: Questions of competing obligations resist precise answers due to variations in magnitude and necessity. Generally one should repay debts before doing new favors -- but not always. A man should ransom his father over his rescuer if he must choose. When the alternative is exceedingly noble or necessary, it takes priority over strict repayment. Context matters: returning an equivalent is not always fair, since the moral character of the parties affects the justice of the exchange. As always, discussions about feelings and actions can only be as precise as their subject matter permits.
summary: While debts should generally be repaid before doing new favors, exceptions arise when an alternative action is exceedingly noble or necessary -- such as ransoming one's father over one's rescuer. The moral character of the parties involved also affects what counts as fair. Ethical discussions about such matters can only be as precise as the subject allows.
distilled: Debts should generally be repaid first, but when an alternative action is far nobler or more necessary, it can rightly take priority -- for ethical judgment must be sensitive to context.
notes: The ransom scenario was a real concern in the ancient Mediterranean, where piracy and brigandage were common. -- 'Discussions about feelings and actions have just as much definiteness as their subject-matter' restates the methodological principle from Book I.3 (1094b12-27) about the precision appropriate to ethics. -- The flexibility Aristotle demands here contrasts with more rigid deontological approaches that would emerge later.

refashioned:
That we should not give every person the same treatment, nor give our father the preference in everything -- just as we do not sacrifice everything to Zeus -- is clear enough. But since we owe different things to parents, brothers, companions, and benefactors, we should render to each class what is appropriate and fitting.

And this is what people actually seem to do. To weddings they invite their relatives, since relatives share in the family and therefore in the affairs that concern the family. At funerals too, people think that relatives above all should attend, for the same reason. In the matter of sustenance, it would seem we should help our parents before all others, since we owe our own nourishment to them, and it is more honorable to help the authors of our being in this way -- even before ourselves.

Honor too one should give to one's parents, as one does to the gods -- but not any and every honor. One should not give the same honor to one's father as to one's mother, nor should one give them the honor due to a philosopher or a general, but rather the honor appropriate to a father or a mother respectively.

To all older persons one should give the honor appropriate to their age -- rising to receive them, offering them seats, and so on. With companions and brothers, one should allow freedom of speech and share everything in common. To relatives, fellow tribesmen, fellow citizens, and every other group, one should always try to give what is appropriate, comparing the claims of each class with respect to closeness of relationship, virtue, and usefulness. The comparison is easier when the people belong to the same class, and more difficult when they are from different classes. Yet we must not on that account shrink from the task, but decide the question as best we can.
elegant:
We do not give every person the same treatment, nor do we sacrifice everything to Zeus. Different relationships demand different things: relatives are invited to weddings and funerals, parents deserve our support in sustenance above all, and honor should be given as appropriate to each role -- not the same honor to a father as to a general or philosopher.

Older persons deserve respect suited to their age; companions deserve frank speech and shared resources. With all groups, we should assign what is appropriate by weighing closeness of relationship, virtue, and usefulness. Comparing claims across different classes of relationship is harder than within them, but we must not shrink from the task.
summary: Different relationships require different forms of respect and support. Parents deserve priority in sustenance, each person deserves honor appropriate to their role, and claims should be weighed by closeness, virtue, and usefulness. Though comparing obligations across different types of relationships is difficult, we must make the best judgment we can.
distilled: We owe different things to different people -- parents, friends, elders, fellow citizens -- and must render to each what is appropriate to their particular relationship and role.
notes: The comparison with Zeus illustrates the Greek principle that even divine worship has proper limits and forms. -- The mention of inviting kinsfolk to marriages and funerals reflects Athenian social customs, where family participation in such events had legal and religious significance. -- The categories (parents, brothers, companions, fellow tribesmen, fellow citizens) map onto the concentric circles of Greek social organization. -- This passage anticipates the Stoic theory of oikeiosis (circles of concern), later developed by Hierocles.

refashioned:
Another question arises: Should friendships be broken off when the other party changes and is no longer the same person? Perhaps we can say that there is nothing strange about ending a friendship based on utility or pleasure when our friends no longer possess those qualities. For it was those qualities that made us friends, and when they disappear, it is reasonable to stop caring for the person.

But one might justly complain of someone who, while actually loving us for our usefulness or pleasantness, pretended to love us for our character. For, as we said at the outset, most conflicts between friends arise when the friendship is not of the kind they believe it to be. So when a person has deceived himself into thinking he was loved for his character, when the other was doing nothing of the sort, he must blame himself. But when he has been deceived by the other person's pretenses, it is right that he should complain against the deceiver -- and with more justice than one complains against counterfeiters of currency, since the wrongdoing involves something far more valuable.
elegant: There is nothing wrong with ending a friendship based on utility or pleasure once those qualities disappear -- we were friends with those qualities, not the person as such. But one may justly complain against someone who pretended to love us for our character while really valuing only our usefulness or pleasantness. Most conflicts arise when friends misunderstand what kind of friendship they have. Deceiving someone about the basis of friendship is worse than counterfeiting currency, since what is at stake is more valuable.
summary: Friendships based on utility or pleasure may reasonably end when those qualities fade. But pretending to love someone for their character while actually valuing their usefulness is a form of deception worse than counterfeiting, because what is at stake -- genuine friendship -- is more valuable than money.
distilled: It is reasonable to end a friendship when its basis -- utility or pleasure -- disappears, but feigning character-based friendship while pursuing mere advantage is a deception worse than counterfeiting currency.
notes: The counterfeiting analogy is striking: debasing coinage was a serious offense in Greek city-states, and Aristotle uses it to emphasize that friendship fraud is even worse. -- The passage recalls Aristotle's distinction among three types of friendship in VIII.3. -- 'Most differences arise between friends when they are not friends in the spirit in which they think they are' is one of Aristotle's most psychologically acute observations about relationships.

refashioned:
But what if one accepts another person as good, and that person turns out badly and is seen to be so -- must one still love him? Surely it is impossible, since not everything can be loved, but only what is good. What is evil neither can nor should be loved, for it is not one's duty to be a lover of evil, nor to make oneself like what is bad. And we have said that like is drawn to like.

Must the friendship, then, be immediately broken off? Or not in all cases, but only when one's friend is incurable in his wickedness? If a friend is capable of being reformed, one should rather come to the aid of his character or his material situation, since this is better and more characteristic of true friendship. But a person who ends such a friendship would not seem to be doing anything strange, for it was not a person of this sort that he befriended. When a friend has changed and one is unable to save him, one lets him go.
elegant: If a friend turns out to be bad, must one still love him? No -- only what is good can be loved, and one should not become like what is bad. But the friendship need not be immediately dissolved: if the friend can be reformed, one should try to help his character, since that is what true friendship demands. Only when the friend is incurably wicked is it right to let him go, for the friendship was with a different person than who he has become.
summary: When a friend turns bad, we are not obligated to continue loving him, since only what is good deserves love. However, if reform is possible, a true friend should try to help. Only when the friend is incurably wicked should the friendship be dissolved -- because the person one befriended no longer exists.
distilled: A true friend tries to help a friend who has gone astray, but when someone becomes incurably wicked, the person one befriended has effectively ceased to exist, and letting go is justified.
notes: The principle that 'like is drawn to like' (to homoion to homoio philon) echoes a traditional Greek maxim found in Plato's Lysis 214a and elsewhere. -- The distinction between curable and incurable vice connects to Aristotle's discussion of voluntary vs. involuntary action in Book III. -- This passage has been influential in Christian ethics regarding the obligation to 'hate the sin but love the sinner.'

refashioned: But what if one friend remained the same while the other became better and far surpassed him in virtue -- should the more virtuous one still treat the other as a friend? Surely not -- at least not in the fullest sense. When the gap becomes great, this is most obvious. Consider childhood friendships: if one friend remained a child in intellect while the other became a fully developed person, how could they be friends when they neither approve of the same things nor take delight in and are pained by the same things? They will not even agree in their feelings toward each other, and without that, as we saw, they cannot be friends -- for they cannot live together. But we have already discussed these matters.
elegant: When one friend grows far beyond the other in virtue, the friendship cannot be maintained in its original form. This is clearest with childhood friendships: if one person matures while the other remains childish in understanding, they can no longer share the same values, pleasures, or pains -- and without these shared experiences, friendship and shared life become impossible.
summary: When one friend significantly outgrows the other in virtue and understanding, the friendship cannot be sustained. Without shared values, pleasures, and the ability to live together, the basis for friendship disappears.
distilled: Friendship requires rough equality in virtue -- when one person far outgrows the other, the shared values and pleasures that sustain friendship inevitably disappear.
notes: Cross-reference to Book VIII.5 (1157a-b) on the requirement that friends share pleasures and activities. -- The childhood friendship example illustrates Aristotle's developmental view of human nature, central to his ethical theory. -- The claim that friends must be able to 'live together' (suzēn) is one of the key criteria of friendship repeatedly emphasized in Books VIII-IX.

refashioned: Should one, then, treat a former friend no differently than someone who was never a friend at all? Surely not. One should preserve a remembrance of the former intimacy. And just as we think we ought to do more for friends than for strangers, so too we ought to make some allowance for those who were once our friends, on account of that former friendship -- provided the break was not caused by extreme wickedness.
elegant: A former friend should not be treated as a mere stranger. The memory of past intimacy deserves respect, and we owe some special consideration to those who were once our friends -- unless the breach was caused by extreme wickedness.
summary: Former friends deserve more consideration than strangers. Past intimacy creates a lasting obligation to show some goodwill, unless the friendship ended due to extreme moral failure.
distilled: Past friendship creates an enduring claim to goodwill -- former friends deserve more than strangers, unless extreme wickedness caused the break.
notes: This brief passage completes the discussion of dissolving friendships (IX.3) with a characteristically Aristotelian sense of moderation: even ended friendships leave moral residue. -- The qualification about 'excess of wickedness' connects to the curable/incurable distinction in 9.10.

refashioned: The friendly feelings we have toward our neighbors, and the marks by which friendships are defined, seem to have originated from a person's relationship to himself. For we define a friend as (1) one who wishes and does what is good, or what seems good, for the sake of his friend; or (2) one who wishes his friend to exist and live, for the friend's own sake -- which is what mothers feel toward their children, and what friends feel who have come into conflict with each other. Others define a friend as (3) one who spends time with another and (4) shares the same tastes, or (5) one who grieves and rejoices with his friend -- and this too is found most of all in mothers. It is by some one of these characteristics that friendship itself is defined.
elegant: Friendly feelings toward others originate from one's relationship with oneself. A friend is defined as someone who: wishes and does good for the other's sake; wishes the other to exist and live; spends time together; shares the same tastes; or grieves and rejoices together. These qualities are found most purely in mothers toward their children, and each captures an essential aspect of friendship.
summary: Aristotle identifies five defining marks of friendship -- wishing good for another, wanting them to exist, spending time together, sharing tastes, and sharing emotions -- and argues these all originate from one's relationship with oneself. Mothers exemplify these qualities most fully.
distilled: The defining marks of friendship -- wishing good, wanting the other to exist, sharing time and tastes, and sharing emotions -- all derive from one's relationship with oneself.
notes: This passage begins IX.4, one of the most important chapters in the Ethics, which grounds friendship in self-relation. -- The five criteria of friendship listed here become the framework for the subsequent argument. -- The emphasis on mothers as exemplars of friendship is notable in the context of ancient Greek philosophy, which often marginalizes women's experiences. -- Compare Plato's Lysis for a different approach to defining friendship.

refashioned:
Now each of these characteristics is true of the good person's relationship to himself -- and of other people insofar as they believe themselves to be good. Virtue and the good person seem, as we have said, to be the standard by which everything of this kind is measured.

For the good person's judgments are harmonious, and he desires the same things with his whole soul. Therefore he wishes for himself what is good and what appears good, and he does it -- for it is characteristic of the good person to work out the good -- and he does so for his own sake, that is, for the sake of the thinking part of himself, which is considered to be the person's true self. He wishes himself to live and to be preserved, and especially that element in him by which he thinks. For existence is good for the virtuous person, and everyone wishes for himself what is good -- though no one would choose to possess the whole world at the price of becoming someone else (for as things stand, God already possesses the good). Rather, each person wishes for good things on the condition of remaining whoever he is. And the element that thinks would seem to be the individual person, or to be so more than any other element in him.

Such a person also wishes to spend time with himself, and does so with pleasure, since the memories of his past actions are delightful and his hopes for the future are good -- and therefore pleasant. His mind is well stocked, too, with subjects for contemplation. And he grieves and rejoices with himself more than anyone else does, for the same thing is always painful to him and the same thing always pleasant -- not one thing at one time and another at another. He has, so to speak, nothing to regret.
elegant:
Every mark of friendship applies to the good person's relation to himself. His judgments are harmonious and his desires unified; he wishes and pursues the good for the sake of his rational self -- which is his truest self. He wishes to live and be preserved, especially the thinking element in him, since existence is good for the virtuous. No one would choose to possess everything at the cost of becoming someone else.

The good person enjoys his own company because his memories are pleasant, his hopes are good, and his mind is rich in contemplation. He grieves and rejoices consistently, always responding the same way to the same things. He has nothing to regret.
summary: All five marks of friendship apply to the good person's relationship with himself. His soul is unified, he pursues the good for the sake of his rational nature, and he enjoys his own company because his memories are pleasant, his hopes bright, and his responses consistent. The thinking element is the truest self, and no one would choose all the goods in the world at the price of becoming someone else.
distilled: The good person is a friend to himself: his soul is unified, his rational nature is his true self, and he enjoys his own company because he has nothing to regret.
notes: The identification of the 'thinking element' (to nooun) with the true self is one of Aristotle's most important claims, developed further in Book X.7-8 where contemplation is identified as the highest happiness. -- The argument that no one would choose to become someone else to gain all goods connects to personal identity -- a theme later central in Locke and Parfit. -- 'God already possesses the good' reflects Aristotle's theology of the unmoved mover (Metaphysics XII). -- The unity of the good person's soul contrasts with the divided soul of the akratic (incontinent) person discussed in Book VII.

refashioned:
Therefore, since each of these characteristics of friendship belongs to the good person in relation to himself, and since he relates to his friend as to himself -- for a friend is another self -- friendship too is thought to involve one or more of these qualities, and those who possess them are thought to be friends.

Whether there can be friendship between a person and himself is a question we may set aside for now. There would seem to be such friendship insofar as a person is, in a sense, two or more parts -- to judge from the characteristics of friendship we have described, and from the fact that the highest degree of friendship is compared to one's love for oneself.
elegant: Since the marks of friendship all belong to the good person's self-relation, and since a friend is another self, friendship is essentially an extension of self-love. Whether one can truly befriend oneself remains an open question, but insofar as a person contains multiple parts, something like self-friendship seems possible -- and the highest friendship is indeed compared to self-love.
summary: Since all the defining characteristics of friendship describe the good person's relationship with himself, and a friend is 'another self,' friendship is an extension of self-relation. The deepest friendship mirrors self-love.
distilled: A friend is another self, and friendship is ultimately an extension of the good person's unified, harmonious relationship with himself.
notes: The phrase 'another self' (allos autos, or heteros autos) is one of the most famous formulations in the Ethics and has been enormously influential in Western thought. -- The puzzle about self-friendship arises from the soul's composite nature (rational and non-rational parts, as discussed in Book I.13). -- This concept anticipates Hegel's analysis of recognition and the self-other dialectic.

refashioned:
But the qualities we described seem to belong even to the majority of people, flawed though they may be. Do we say, then, that insofar as they are satisfied with themselves and believe themselves good, they share in these qualities? Certainly no one who is thoroughly bad and impious possesses them, or even appears to.

These qualities scarcely belong even to inferior people, for they are at war with themselves. They have appetites pulling them toward some things and rational desires toward others. This is the case, for instance, with people who lack self-control: they choose what is merely pleasant but harmful instead of what they themselves judge to be good. Others, through cowardice and laziness, shrink from doing what they believe is best for themselves.

Those who have committed many terrible deeds and are hated for their wickedness even shrink from life and destroy themselves. Wicked people seek out the company of others and avoid being alone, for when by themselves they remember many grievous deeds and anticipate more like them, but in company they can forget. Having nothing lovable in themselves, they feel no love for themselves.

Such people do not even share in their own joys and griefs, for their soul is torn by internal conflict: one part, by reason of its wickedness, grieves when it abstains from certain acts, while another part is pleased; one element pulls them this way and another pulls them that, as if tearing them apart. If a person cannot feel pain and pleasure at the same time, at the very least he soon feels pain because he felt pleasure, and wishes those things had never been pleasant to him. For bad people are weighed down with regret.
elegant:
The marks of friendship with oneself barely belong to ordinary people and not at all to the thoroughly wicked. Inferior people are at war with themselves: their appetites pull one way, their reason another. The incontinent choose harmful pleasures over what they know to be good; the cowardly and lazy fail to do what they believe is best.

The worst become so self-alienated that they shrink from life itself. They avoid solitude because it forces them to face their own deeds, and seek company to forget. Having nothing lovable in themselves, they cannot love themselves. Their soul is torn by faction -- one part grieving while another rejoices -- and they are weighed down with regret.
summary: The self-friendship that characterizes the good person is absent in the wicked. Bad people are internally divided: their appetites conflict with their reason, they avoid solitude because it confronts them with their own deeds, and they feel no genuine self-love. Their souls are torn by faction, and they are burdened with constant regret.
distilled: The wicked person cannot be a friend to himself because his soul is at war -- divided between appetite and reason, haunted by past deeds, and burdened with regret.
notes: The description of the wicked person's inner conflict is one of Aristotle's most powerful psychological portraits. -- The reference to incontinent (akratic) people connects to the extensive discussion in Book VII.1-10. -- 'Their soul is rent by faction' uses political language (stasis = civil strife), consistent with Aristotle's analogy between the soul and the city. -- The observation that the wicked 'shrink from life and destroy themselves' may reflect awareness of suicide in the Greek world, discussed also in Book V.11 (1138a). -- Compare Plato's Republic IV (435-441) on the tripartite soul and inner conflict.

refashioned: Therefore the bad person does not seem to be on friendly terms even with himself, because there is nothing in him to love. If being in such a condition is the height of wretchedness, we should strain every nerve to avoid wickedness and endeavor to be good. For only in this way can one be either a friend to oneself or a friend to another.
elegant: The bad person cannot befriend even himself, since there is nothing in him to love. If this self-alienation is the height of misery, we must strive to be good -- for only goodness makes possible friendship with oneself and with others.
summary: The bad person cannot love himself because there is nothing lovable in him. Since this inner alienation is the worst form of wretchedness, becoming good is essential -- it is the only path to friendship with oneself and with others.
distilled: Only the good person can be a friend to himself, and self-friendship is the necessary foundation for all other friendship.
notes: This passage concludes IX.4 with a powerful exhortation that links self-love, virtue, and friendship into a single ethical imperative. -- The argument that wickedness is self-defeating (one cannot even be a friend to oneself) is distinctively Aristotelian and contrasts with purely external accounts of the harm of vice. -- Compare Plato's Gorgias (469-479) where Socrates argues that doing wrong harms the wrongdoer more than anyone.

refashioned: Goodwill is a kind of friendly feeling, but it is not the same as friendship. For one can feel goodwill toward people one does not know, and without their knowing it -- but friendship requires mutual awareness. This has already been said. But goodwill is not even the same as affection. For it lacks the intensity and desire that accompany affection; affection implies intimacy, while goodwill can arise suddenly. This happens, for example, with competitors in a contest: we come to feel goodwill toward them and share in their wishes, but we would not actually do anything with them. As we said, we feel goodwill suddenly and care for them only superficially.
elegant: Goodwill resembles friendship but is not the same thing. It can exist without mutual awareness, lacks the intensity and desire of genuine affection, and requires no intimacy. Goodwill can arise suddenly -- as when we root for competitors in a contest -- but remains superficial: we share their wishes without acting on their behalf.
summary: Goodwill is a friendly feeling that falls short of friendship. Unlike friendship, it requires no mutual awareness, lacks intensity and desire, and can arise suddenly -- as when we feel sympathy for competitors. It involves wishing well without any commitment to act.
distilled: Goodwill is a friendly wish for another's good that arises easily and requires neither intimacy nor action -- making it friendship's beginning but not friendship itself.
notes: Eunoia (goodwill) is distinguished from philia (friendship) and philēsis (affection/friendly feeling). -- The contest example likely refers to athletic competitions at Greek festivals (Olympic, Isthmian, etc.), where spectators would develop sudden sympathies for unknown competitors. -- This section (IX.5) begins Aristotle's analysis of friendship-adjacent phenomena: goodwill, concord, and beneficence.

refashioned:
Goodwill seems, then, to be the beginning of friendship, just as the pleasure of the eye is the beginning of romantic love. For no one falls in love without first being delighted by the beloved's appearance; but someone who delights in another's appearance does not thereby love that person -- only when he also longs for the absent beloved and craves the beloved's presence. In the same way, it is not possible for people to be friends without first feeling goodwill toward each other, but those who feel goodwill are not yet friends: they merely wish well to those for whom they feel it, and would not actually do anything with them or take trouble for them.

By extending the term, one might call goodwill an inactive friendship. But when goodwill is prolonged and reaches the point of intimacy, it becomes friendship -- not friendship based on utility or pleasure, since goodwill does not arise on those terms either. A person who has received a benefit returns goodwill for what has been done for him, which is simply doing what is just. But someone who wishes another well because he hopes to be enriched through that person has goodwill not toward the other but toward himself -- just as a person is not truly a friend to another if he values him only for the use he can get from him.

In general, goodwill arises on account of some excellence and worth -- when one person seems to another to be admirable, brave, or something of the sort, as we described in the case of competitors in a contest.
elegant:
Goodwill is the beginning of friendship as visual delight is the beginning of love: necessary but not sufficient. Goodwill becomes friendship only when prolonged into intimacy -- and only character-based friendship, not that based on utility or pleasure. Goodwill that hopes for personal enrichment is really self-directed.

Genuine goodwill arises from recognizing excellence in another -- their virtue, courage, or admirable qualities.
summary: Goodwill is to friendship what visual attraction is to love: a necessary beginning but not the thing itself. Goodwill becomes friendship only through prolonged intimacy, and only the character-based kind. Goodwill motivated by expected personal benefit is really self-interest in disguise. Genuine goodwill arises from recognizing excellence in another person.
distilled: Goodwill is the seed of friendship -- arising from recognizing another's excellence -- but it becomes true friendship only through sustained intimacy and genuine concern for the other's good.
notes: The analogy between goodwill/friendship and visual attraction/love is one of Aristotle's most elegant comparisons. -- The distinction between genuine goodwill and self-interested well-wishing anticipates Kant's distinction between acting from duty and acting from inclination. -- 'Inactive friendship' (argē philia) is a striking phrase that captures goodwill's potential quality. -- The emphasis on excellence (aretē) as the trigger for goodwill reinforces the primacy of virtue-based friendship.

refashioned:
Concord also seems to be a friendly relation. For this reason, it is not merely identity of opinion: people who do not know each other can hold the same views. Nor do we call people who agree on any and every subject concordant -- for instance, those who agree about astronomical matters, since agreement about such things is not a friendly relation.

Rather, we say a city is concordant when its citizens share the same opinion about what is in their interest, choose the same course of action, and carry out what they have resolved in common. Concord, therefore, concerns matters of action -- and specifically matters of consequence in which it is possible for all parties to get what they want.

For example, a city is concordant when all its citizens think that public offices should be elected, or that they should form an alliance with Sparta, or that Pittacus should be their ruler -- at a time when Pittacus himself was also willing to rule. But when each of two parties wants the position for himself -- like the rival brothers in the Phoenician Women -- they are in a state of faction. For concord does not mean merely thinking of the same thing, whatever it may be, but thinking of it in the same hands. For instance, when both the common people and the upper class wish the best men to rule, all get what they aim at.

Concord, then, seems to be political friendship, as it is commonly said to be, for it concerns things that are in our interest and have an influence on our lives.
elegant: Concord is not mere agreement in opinion but shared commitment to action in matters of public interest. A city is concordant when citizens agree on what serves the common good, choose the same policies, and carry them out together. When each faction wants power for itself -- like the rival brothers in the Phoenician Women -- the result is strife, not concord. True concord requires wanting the same outcome in the same hands: when all agree that the best should rule, everyone gets what they want. Concord is thus political friendship -- agreement about how to live together.
summary: Concord is not merely holding the same opinions but sharing practical commitments about matters of public interest. A city enjoys concord when citizens agree on policies and execute them together. When factions compete for power, concord breaks down. True concord -- political friendship -- exists when all agree that the best should govern.
distilled: Concord is political friendship: not agreement in theory but shared commitment to common action and common good.
notes: Homonoia (concord/unanimity) is analyzed as a political concept, connecting to Aristotle's Politics where civic friendship (politikē philia) is essential to the polis. -- Pittacus of Mytilene (c. 640-568 BC) was one of the Seven Sages of Greece and served as elected tyrant (aisymnētēs) of Mytilene for ten years before voluntarily stepping down. -- The Phoenician Women (Phoinissai) is a tragedy by Euripides depicting the conflict between Eteocles and Polynices, sons of Oedipus, over the kingship of Thebes. -- Sparta's alliance politics were a live issue throughout the 4th century BC. -- The distinction between wanting the same thing 'in the same hands' vs. 'for oneself' is a subtle and important political insight.

refashioned:
Such concord is found among good people, for they are concordant both within themselves and with one another. Being of one mind, so to speak -- since their wishes are stable and not subject to opposing currents like a strait of the sea -- they wish for what is just and what is advantageous, and these are the objects of their common endeavor as well.

But bad people cannot achieve concord except to a small extent, just as they can scarcely be friends. They aim at getting more than their share of advantages while doing less than their share of the labor and public service. And since each person pursues his own advantage, he scrutinizes and obstructs his neighbor. If people do not watch carefully over the common good, it is soon destroyed. The result is that they fall into faction, each trying to compel the others while being unwilling to do what is just themselves.
elegant: Good people achieve concord because their wishes are stable and directed toward justice and the common advantage. Bad people cannot sustain concord: they grab more than their share of benefits while shirking labor and public service. Each pursues private advantage, obstructs others, and neglects the common good -- resulting in faction and mutual compulsion.
summary: Good people achieve concord because their desires are stable and directed toward justice. Bad people cannot maintain concord because each seeks personal advantage while shirking shared responsibilities, leading to faction and the destruction of the common good.
distilled: Concord flourishes among the good because they pursue justice together; among the bad, it collapses into faction as each grabs advantages while shirking duties.
notes: The image of wishes 'at the mercy of opposing currents like a strait of the sea' likely refers to the Euripus, the narrow strait between Euboea and mainland Greece known for its unpredictable reversing currents. -- The analysis of faction (stasis) among the bad mirrors Aristotle's discussion of political instability in Politics V. -- The free-rider problem Aristotle identifies -- seeking more benefits while contributing less -- is a classic collective action problem analyzed in modern political science and game theory.

refashioned:
Benefactors are thought to love those they have benefited more than the recipients love those who treated them well, and this is discussed as though it were paradoxical. Most people explain it by analogy with debts: just as debtors wish their creditors did not exist while creditors take care to preserve their debtors, so benefactors want the objects of their generosity to survive in order to receive gratitude, while beneficiaries take no interest in making this return.

Epicharmus would perhaps say that people who think this way are simply "looking at things from their bad side." But it is quite true to human nature, for most people are forgetful and more eager to receive benefits than to give them.

However, the real cause seems to be more deeply rooted in the nature of things, and the analogy with moneylenders is not really apt. Creditors have no friendly feeling toward their debtors -- only a wish that they survive so the debt can be collected. But those who have benefited others feel genuine friendship and love for those they have helped, even if those people are of no use to them and never will be.

This is what happens with craftsmen too: every craftsman loves his own work more than that work would love him if it came alive. And this is perhaps truest of all with poets, who have an extravagant love for their own poems, doting on them as if they were their children.

This resembles the position of benefactors: the person they have helped is their handiwork, and so they love this handiwork more than the handiwork loves its maker. The reason is that existence is something all people value and love, and that we exist through activity -- through living and acting. The handiwork is, in a sense, the maker expressed in activity. The maker loves his handiwork, therefore, because he loves existence. And this is rooted in the nature of things: what he is in potential, his handiwork manifests in actuality.
elegant:
Benefactors love those they benefit more than recipients love their benefactors -- and the reason goes deeper than the debtor-creditor analogy suggests. Creditors merely want debtors to survive to repay; benefactors feel genuine love because those they help are their handiwork.

Every craftsman loves his creation -- poets most of all, doting on their works like children. Similarly, benefactors love the recipients as expressions of themselves in action. We exist through activity, and what we are in potential, our works manifest in actuality. The benefactor loves his handiwork because he loves his own existence expressed through it.
summary: Benefactors love those they help more than recipients love their benefactors -- not because of expected gratitude, but because every maker loves his handiwork. The person benefited is the benefactor's creation, manifesting in actuality what the benefactor is in potential. We love our works because through them we express and extend our own existence.
distilled: Benefactors love those they help more than they are loved in return, because every maker loves his own handiwork -- it expresses in actuality what he is in potential.
notes: Epicharmus of Kos (c. 540-450 BC): Sicilian comic playwright, often credited with founding Greek comedy; only fragments survive. His quip about 'looking at things on their bad side' reflects his reputation for witty moralizing. -- The craftsman analogy connects to Aristotle's theory of the four causes (Metaphysics I, Physics II): the efficient cause (maker) takes pleasure in seeing its product (formal and final cause actualized). -- The potentiality/actuality (dynamis/energeia) distinction is one of Aristotle's most fundamental metaphysical concepts, here applied to human psychology. -- The comparison of poets with parents who dote on children anticipates Romantic notions of artistic creation as self-expression.

refashioned:
At the same time, for the benefactor there is something noble in what depends on his action, so that he takes delight in the person he has helped. But for the recipient, there is nothing noble in the benefactor himself -- at most something advantageous, and this is less pleasant and less worthy of love.

What is pleasant comes in three forms: the activity of the present, the hope of the future, and the memory of the past. But most pleasant of all is what depends on our own activity, and this is also most lovable. Now for the person who has created something, his work endures -- for what is noble is lasting -- but for the person who received the benefit, the usefulness passes away. And the memory of noble deeds is pleasant, while the memory of useful things received is unlikely to be pleasant, or less so -- though when it comes to expectation, the reverse seems to hold true.
elegant: The benefactor finds nobility and lasting delight in his action, while the recipient gains only temporary advantage. Present activity, future hope, and past memory are all pleasant -- but our own activity is most pleasant of all. The benefactor's noble work endures in memory; the recipient's utility fades. Though the memory of noble deeds is sweet and that of received benefits less so, expectation works in reverse: we look forward more to receiving than to giving.
summary: Benefactors find lasting pleasure in the nobility of their actions, while recipients gain only temporary advantage. Our own activity gives the deepest pleasure, and noble deeds endure in memory while received benefits fade. However, when it comes to anticipation, receiving benefits is more eagerly expected than giving them.
distilled: The pleasure of giving endures because noble action lives on in memory, while the utility of receiving fades -- though we anticipate receiving more eagerly than giving.
notes: The tripartite division of pleasure into present activity, future hope, and past memory is also found in Plato's Philebus (36a). -- The distinction between the lasting pleasure of noble action and the transient pleasure of utility reinforces Aristotle's hierarchy of goods (Book I). -- The observation that expectation reverses the pattern (we look forward to receiving more than giving) shows Aristotle's psychological realism.

refashioned:
Furthermore, loving is like activity, while being loved is like passivity; and the impulse to love, together with the feelings that accompany it, belongs to those who are more active.

Again, all people love more what they have won through effort. Those who have earned their money love it more than those who inherited it, and being helped seems to require no effort, while helping others is hard work.

These are also the reasons why mothers are fonder of their children than fathers are: bringing children into the world costs them more pain, and they know more certainly that the children are their own. This last point would seem to apply to benefactors as well.
elegant: Loving is activity, being loved is passivity -- and we are naturally drawn to activity. We love more what we have won through effort: the self-made love their wealth more than heirs do. Mothers love their children more than fathers because childbirth costs them more pain and gives them greater certainty the children are theirs. The same principle applies to benefactors, who invest more effort than recipients.
summary: Loving is active while being loved is passive, and we value more what we achieve through effort. This explains why mothers love their children more than fathers do -- childbirth requires greater labor and gives greater certainty of parentage. The same principle explains why benefactors love more than beneficiaries.
distilled: We love most what costs us the most effort -- which is why mothers love their children more than fathers, and benefactors love more than those they benefit.
notes: The active/passive (poiesis/pathesis) distinction reflects Aristotle's Categories and Physics. -- The observation about mothers' greater attachment due to the labor of childbirth is remarkably empirical for ancient philosophy. -- The claim about certainty of parentage ('they know better that the children are their own') reflects the ancient world's concern with paternity certainty, which had legal implications for inheritance and citizenship in Athens. -- The 'effort justification' principle Aristotle describes is well-documented in modern social psychology (the 'IKEA effect').

refashioned: The question is also debated whether a person should love himself most, or someone else. People criticize those who love themselves above all and call them "self-lovers," using this as a term of reproach. The bad person, it is thought, does everything for his own sake -- and the more wicked he is, the more he does so. People reproach him, for instance, for never doing anything unless it serves his own interest. The good person, by contrast, acts for the sake of what is noble, and the better he is, the more he does so. He acts for his friend's sake and sacrifices his own interest.
elegant: Whether one should love oneself most is debated. 'Self-lover' is used as a term of reproach, since bad people seem to do everything for their own sake. The good person, by contrast, acts for what is noble and for his friend's sake, sacrificing personal interest.
summary: Common opinion treats 'self-lover' as an insult, since bad people are thought to do everything for their own benefit. Good people, by contrast, act for what is noble and sacrifice their own interests for friends.
distilled: Common opinion condemns self-love because bad people act selfishly, while good people sacrifice their own interests for what is noble.
notes: This passage opens IX.8, one of the most philosophically rich chapters in the Ethics, which rehabilitates a proper form of self-love. -- The Greek term philautos (self-lover) was pejorative in common usage, as Aristotle notes. -- The tension between self-love and altruism that Aristotle sets up here remains central to ethical theory, from the Stoics through Christianity to modern debates between egoism and altruism.

refashioned:
But the facts clash with these arguments, and this is not surprising. For people say that one ought to love one's best friend most, and a person's best friend is the one who wishes well to the object of his care for that person's own sake, even if no one will ever know of it. These qualities are found most of all in a person's attitude toward himself -- as are all the other qualities by which a friend is defined. For, as we have said, it is from one's relationship with oneself that all the characteristics of friendship extend to others.

All the proverbs agree with this too: "a single soul," "what friends have is common property," "friendship is equality," and "charity begins at home." All these marks are found most fully in a person's relation to himself: he is his own best friend and therefore ought to love himself most.

It is, then, a reasonable question which of the two views we should follow, for both are plausible.
elegant: Yet the facts contradict the common reproach of self-love. One's best friend is defined as whoever wishes one well for one's own sake, even anonymously -- and these qualities are found most fully in one's relation to oneself. All proverbs confirm this: 'a single soul,' 'friends share everything,' 'friendship is equality,' 'charity begins at home.' A person is his own best friend. Both views -- that self-love is shameful and that it is natural -- are plausible, creating a genuine puzzle.
summary: Despite the common reproach of self-love, the defining qualities of friendship -- wishing someone well for their own sake, sharing everything, being of one soul -- are found most fully in one's relationship with oneself. Proverbs confirm that a person is his own best friend. Both the case for and against self-love are plausible.
distilled: By every definition of friendship, a person is his own best friend -- yet self-love is commonly condemned, creating a paradox that demands resolution.
notes: The proverbs cited are well-attested in Greek literature: 'a single soul' (mia psychē) appears in various forms; 'friends share everything' (koina ta philōn) is attributed to Pythagoras by Diogenes Laertius; 'charity begins at home' translates a Greek proverb about the knee being closer than the shin (Theocritus). -- The dialectical method here -- setting up opposing plausible views and then resolving them -- is characteristic of Aristotle's procedure throughout the Ethics (cf. Book VII.1 on akrasia). -- The argument that friendship's criteria point back to self-relation picks up from IX.4 (9.13-9.15).

refashioned:
Perhaps we should distinguish these arguments from each other and determine how far and in what respects each view is correct. If we grasp the sense in which each side uses the phrase "lover of self," the truth may become clear.

Those who use the term as a reproach apply it to people who assign to themselves the greater share of wealth, honors, and bodily pleasures. These are what most people desire and compete for, treating them as though they were the highest goods. So those who are grasping with regard to these things gratify their appetites and, in general, the irrational element of their soul. Since most people are of this nature, the term "self-lover" has acquired its negative meaning from this prevailing type of self-love, which is indeed a bad one. It is just, therefore, that people who love themselves in this way are reproached.

That most people ordinarily use "self-lover" for those who give themselves the advantage in such matters is clear enough. For if someone were always anxious above all to act justly, temperately, or in accordance with any other virtue -- if he were always striving to secure for himself the honorable course -- no one would call such a person a "self-lover" or blame him for it.
elegant:
The resolution lies in distinguishing two senses of 'self-lover.' Those rightly reproached as self-lovers are people who grab the largest share of wealth, honors, and bodily pleasures -- gratifying appetite and the irrational soul. Most people are like this, which is why the term has become pejorative.

But if someone strove above all to act justly and virtuously -- always securing for himself the honorable course -- no one would call him a 'self-lover' or blame him. The term condemns one kind of self-love while another kind goes unnamed and unblameable.
summary: The apparent contradiction about self-love dissolves once we distinguish two kinds. The commonly reproached 'self-lover' is someone who grabs the largest share of wealth, honor, and pleasure -- gratifying the irrational soul. But someone who strives above all to act virtuously and honorably would never be called a self-lover, even though he too assigns himself the best things.
distilled: The reproach of self-love applies only to those who serve their appetites -- no one blames a person who 'selfishly' pursues virtue and nobility above all else.
notes: This is the crucial argumentative turn of IX.8, where Aristotle resolves the paradox by distinguishing between two types of self-love. -- The distinction between rational and irrational elements of the soul draws on the soul's division established in Book I.13. -- The 'objects of competition' (wealth, honors, bodily pleasures) correspond to the goods pursued by the three inferior lives discussed in Book I.5. -- This argument has been deeply influential on subsequent ethical thought, informing debates about whether true self-interest and morality can coincide.

refashioned:
But such a person would actually seem to be a lover of self in a deeper sense. At any rate, he assigns to himself the things that are noblest and best, and he gratifies the most authoritative element in himself, obeying it in all things. Just as a city or any other organized whole is most properly identified with its most authoritative element, so too is a person. Therefore the person who loves this authoritative element and gratifies it is most of all a lover of self.

Moreover, a person is said to have or lack self-control depending on whether his reason is or is not in command -- on the assumption that reason is the person's true self. And the things people have done on a rational principle are considered most properly their own acts and voluntary acts. That this rational element is the person himself -- or is so more than anything else -- is clear. And it is clear too that the good person loves this element most.

Therefore the good person is most truly a lover of self -- but of a different type from the one that is a matter of reproach, as different as living according to reason is from living as passion dictates, and as different as desiring what is noble is from desiring what merely seems advantageous.

Those who devote themselves exceptionally to noble actions win universal approval and praise. And if everyone were to strive toward what is noble and strain every nerve to do the noblest deeds, everything would be as it should for the common good, and every individual would secure for himself the greatest of goods -- since virtue is the greatest of goods.
elegant:
The person who pursues nobility and gratifies his reason -- the most authoritative element in him -- is the truest self-lover. Just as a city is identified with its governing element, a person is most properly identified with his rational nature. Self-control means reason is in command, and rational acts are most truly our own.

This noble self-love differs from the vulgar kind as reason differs from passion, and as desiring what is noble differs from chasing apparent advantage. If everyone competed in nobility rather than material goods, both the common good and individual good would be perfectly served -- since virtue is the greatest good.
summary: The good person is the truest self-lover because he gratifies reason, his most authoritative and essential element. This noble self-love is as different from vulgar self-love as reason is from passion. If everyone competed in virtue rather than material goods, both individual and common good would flourish, since virtue is the greatest good.
distilled: True self-love means gratifying reason -- your deepest self -- and if everyone competed to be most virtuous rather than most wealthy, both personal and common good would be perfectly served.
notes: The identification of the self with reason (nous) is developed further in Book X.7 (1177b-1178a), where Aristotle argues that contemplative activity is the most complete happiness because it exercises the most divine element in us. -- The political analogy (the self is to reason as the city is to its governing element) connects to Politics III.6 on constitutions. -- The claim that universal pursuit of virtue would optimize the common good represents Aristotle's version of the harmony between individual and social good -- a question that dominates modern political philosophy from Hobbes to Rawls. -- The distinction between 'what is noble' (to kalon) and 'what seems advantageous' (to dokoun sympherein) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethical framework.

refashioned:
Therefore the good person should be a lover of self -- for he will both profit himself by doing noble acts and benefit his fellows. But the wicked person should not be a lover of self, for he will harm both himself and his neighbors by following evil passions. For the wicked person, what he does clashes with what he ought to do; but what the good person ought to do, he does, since reason in each of its possessors chooses what is best for itself, and the good person obeys his reason.

It is also true that the good person does many things for the sake of his friends and his country, and if necessary dies for them. He will throw away wealth, honors, and all the goods that people compete for, gaining for himself nobility. For he would prefer a short period of intense pleasure to a long one of mild enjoyment, a year of noble life to many years of humdrum existence, and one great and noble action to many trivial ones.

This is perhaps what happens with those who die for others: they choose a great prize for themselves. They will give away wealth too, on the condition that their friends will gain more; for while the friend gains money, the good person gains nobility -- and so he assigns the greater good to himself.

The same is true of honors and public offices -- he will sacrifice all of these to his friend, for this is noble and praiseworthy for himself. Rightly, then, is he considered good, since he chooses nobility above all else. He may even yield opportunities for action to his friend, for it may be nobler to be the cause of his friend's acting than to act himself.

In all the actions for which people are praised, then, the good person is seen to assign to himself the greater share of what is noble. In this sense, as we have said, a person should be a lover of self; but in the sense in which most people are lovers of self, he should not be.
elegant:
The good person should be a self-lover: he benefits himself through noble acts while also benefiting others. The wicked should not, since their passions harm themselves and others alike.

The good person will sacrifice wealth, honors, and even life for friends and country -- choosing a short, noble life over a long, mediocre one, and one great action over many trivial ones. Those who die for others choose the greater prize: nobility. By giving away wealth so friends gain materially while he gains nobility, he assigns himself the greater good.

He may even yield opportunities for action, since being the cause of a friend's noble deed can be nobler than acting oneself. In all praiseworthy actions, the good person claims the larger share of what is noble. One should be a self-lover in this sense -- but not in the vulgar sense.
summary: The good person should love himself because through noble acts he benefits both himself and others. He willingly sacrifices wealth, honor, and even life because he prefers nobility to material goods -- choosing a short but glorious life over a long but mediocre one. By giving away material goods while claiming nobility, he actually assigns himself the greater good. True self-love means claiming the greater share of what is noble, not what is materially advantageous.
distilled: True self-love means choosing nobility over comfort -- even sacrificing life itself -- because the person who claims the greater share of what is noble assigns himself the genuinely greater good.
notes: This is the culmination of IX.8's argument, one of the most celebrated passages in the Ethics. -- The claim that dying for others is a form of self-love (choosing 'a great prize') is paradoxical and profound, reframing sacrifice as the ultimate expression of rational self-interest. -- The preference for 'a short period of intense pleasure to a long one of mild enjoyment' connects to debates about the temporal structure of happiness (cf. Book I.10 on Solon's 'count no man happy until dead'). -- The idea that being the cause of a friend's action can be nobler than acting oneself shows Aristotle's nuanced understanding of moral agency. -- This passage has influenced Christian ethics (kenosis, self-emptying love), Nietzsche's critique of altruism, and contemporary virtue ethics.

refashioned:
It is also debated whether the happy person will need friends or not. It is said that those who are supremely happy and self-sufficient have no need of friends, since they already possess what is good. Being self-sufficient, they need nothing further -- while a friend, being another self, furnishes what a person cannot provide through his own effort. Hence the saying: "When fortune is kind, what need of friends?"

But it seems strange, when we assign all good things to the happy person, not to assign him friends, who are thought to be the greatest of external goods. And if it is more characteristic of a friend to do well by others than to be well treated, and if conferring benefits is characteristic of the good person and of virtue, and if it is nobler to do well by friends than by strangers, then the good person will need people to do well by.

This is why the question is raised whether we need friends more in prosperity or in adversity -- on the assumption that both the person in misfortune who needs help and the prosperous person who needs people to benefit require friends.

Surely it is strange, too, to make the supremely happy person a solitary. For no one would choose to possess the whole world on the condition of being alone, since a human being is a political creature whose nature is to live with others. Therefore even the happy person lives with others, for he possesses the things that are by nature good. And plainly it is better to spend one's days with friends and good people than with strangers or random persons. Therefore the happy person needs friends.
elegant:
Some argue that the supremely happy and self-sufficient need no friends. But it is strange to assign someone all good things while withholding friends -- the greatest external good. If virtue means conferring benefits and it is nobler to benefit friends than strangers, the good person needs friends to benefit.

Making the happy person a solitary is absurd: no one would choose to possess everything in isolation, since humans are by nature political and social. The happy person needs good friends to live with, since it is better to spend one's days with friends than with strangers.
summary: Against the view that the self-sufficient need no friends, Aristotle argues that friends are the greatest external good and essential to happiness. The good person needs friends to benefit, and since humans are inherently social, no one would choose total prosperity in isolation. The happy person needs virtuous friends.
distilled: No one would choose to possess everything in isolation -- the happy person needs friends because humans are social by nature and because virtue itself requires others to benefit.
notes: This passage opens IX.9, which provides Aristotle's most sustained argument for why the happy person needs friends. -- 'When fortune is kind, what need of friends?' is attributed to Euripides (Orestes, line 665 in some editions). -- 'A human being is a political creature' (politikon zōon) echoes the famous statement in Politics I.2 (1253a2-3). -- The tension between self-sufficiency (autarkeia) and the need for friends is one of the central puzzles of the Ethics; self-sufficiency was defined in Book I.7 as 'that which makes life desirable and lacking in nothing.' -- The Epicureans later argued that friendship is essential to happiness despite their emphasis on individual pleasure, partly drawing on this Aristotelian argument.

refashioned: What then does the first school mean, and in what respect is it right? Is it perhaps that most people identify friends with useful people? The supremely happy person will indeed have no need of such friends, since he already possesses what is good. Nor will he need friends sought for the sake of pleasure, or only to a very small extent -- for his life is already pleasant and has no need of externally supplied pleasure. And because he does not need friends of these kinds, he is thought not to need friends at all.
elegant: The claim that the happy person needs no friends is only correct if 'friends' means friends of utility or pleasure. The supremely happy person already has good things and a pleasant life, so he needs neither useful nor amusing companions. But this limited conception of friendship does not exhaust what friendship truly is.
summary: The argument that the happy person needs no friends is only valid if friendship means friends for utility or pleasure. The supremely happy person already has these things. But this overlooks character-based friendship, which serves a deeper need.
distilled: The happy person needs no friends of utility or pleasure, but this does not mean he needs no friends at all -- it means the objection misunderstands what true friendship is.
notes: This passage dissolves the apparent contradiction by applying the three-fold typology of friendship from Book VIII.3. -- The move is characteristic of Aristotle's dialectical method: showing that an apparently strong objection rests on an equivocation.

refashioned:
But that conclusion is surely not true. For we said at the outset that happiness is an activity, and activity is something that comes into being and is not simply present from the start like a piece of property.
