If happiness lies in living and being active, and the good person's activity is virtuous and pleasant in itself, as we said at the outset; and if something's being one's own is one of the things that makes it pleasant; and if we can observe our neighbors better than ourselves and their actions better than our own; and if the actions of virtuous people who are friends are pleasant to good people, since these actions have both naturally pleasant qualities -- being both virtuous and one's own, in a sense -- then the supremely happy person will need friends of this sort. For his purpose is to contemplate worthy actions and actions that are his own, and the actions of a good person who is his friend have both these qualities.
elegant: Happiness is an activity, not a possession, so the happy person needs ongoing virtuous activity. Since we observe others better than ourselves, and the actions of virtuous friends are both inherently pleasant and in a sense our own, the happy person needs good friends. Through them he contemplates worthy actions that belong to him -- fulfilling happiness's requirement for sustained virtuous activity.
summary: Since happiness is an ongoing activity rather than a static possession, the happy person needs virtuous friends. We observe others better than ourselves, and the actions of virtuous friends are both intrinsically pleasant and, in a sense, our own. Through such friends, we can contemplate worthy actions -- which is essential to happiness.
distilled: Happiness is an activity, not a possession -- and since we see our friends' virtuous actions more clearly than our own, good friends are essential for contemplating the worthy actions that constitute a happy life.
notes: The argument that happiness is an activity (energeia) rather than a state (hexis) or possession (ktēsis) is established in Book I.7-8 and is one of Aristotle's most important ethical claims. -- The observation that we can observe others better than ourselves has interesting connections to modern psychology on self-knowledge and bias. -- The idea that a friend's actions are 'one's own in a sense' follows from the 'another self' doctrine (IX.4). -- This passage combines metaphysics (actuality/potentiality), epistemology (self-knowledge), and ethics in a remarkably compressed argument.

refashioned:
Furthermore, people think that the happy person ought to live pleasantly. Now if he were a solitary, life would be difficult for him, since it is not easy to be continuously active by oneself; but with others and in relation to others, it is easier. With others, therefore, his activity will be more continuous -- which is itself pleasant, as it ought to be for the supremely happy person.

For the good person, insofar as he is good, delights in virtuous actions and is troubled by vicious ones, just as a musical person enjoys beautiful melodies but is pained by bad ones. Moreover, a certain training in virtue comes from keeping the company of good people, as Theognis said before us.
elegant: The happy person needs others for sustained activity, since continuous virtuous action is difficult in isolation but easier in company. A good person naturally delights in virtue and is pained by vice, like a musician who enjoys fine melodies and is distressed by poor ones. The company of good people also provides training in virtue, as Theognis observed.
summary: Solitary life makes sustained virtuous activity difficult. With friends, the happy person can maintain continuous activity, which is itself pleasant. Good people naturally enjoy virtuous actions, and the company of other good people provides ongoing training in virtue.
distilled: Sustained virtuous activity -- the core of happiness -- is difficult in isolation but natural in the company of good friends, who both delight and train us in virtue.
notes: Theognis of Megara (6th century BC): elegiac poet whose verses on aristocratic virtue and the company one keeps were widely quoted in Greek education. The relevant passage is likely Theognis 35-36: 'From noble men you will learn noble things; if you mingle with the base, you will lose even the wits you have.' -- The musician analogy is typical of Aristotle's use of craft analogies throughout the Ethics. -- The argument that continuous activity requires company connects to the social nature of human beings (politikon zōon) stressed in the Politics.

refashioned:
If we look more deeply into the nature of things, a virtuous friend seems to be naturally desirable for a virtuous person. For what is good by nature, we have said, is good and pleasant in itself for the virtuous person.

Now life is defined for animals by the power of perception, and for human beings by the power of perception or thought. A power is defined by reference to its corresponding activity, which is the essential thing. Therefore life seems to consist essentially in the act of perceiving or thinking.

Life is among the things that are good and pleasant in themselves, since it is determinate, and the determinate belongs to the nature of the good. What is good by nature is also good for the virtuous person, which is why life seems pleasant to everyone. But we must not apply this to a wicked or corrupt life, nor to a life spent in pain, for such a life is indeterminate, as are its qualities. The nature of pain will become clearer in what follows.
elegant: A virtuous friend is naturally desirable for a virtuous person because what is good by nature is good and pleasant for the virtuous. Life itself -- defined essentially as the activity of perception or thought -- is among things good and pleasant in themselves, since it possesses determinacy, which belongs to the nature of the good. Life is good for the virtuous, though not a wicked or painful life, which lacks this determinacy.
summary: Aristotle argues that a virtuous friend is naturally desirable by building from first principles: life consists essentially in perception and thought, and is inherently good and pleasant because it is determinate. Since what is naturally good is good for the virtuous person, life is pleasant to the virtuous -- though not a wicked or pain-filled life, which lacks determinacy.
distilled: Life itself -- the activity of perceiving and thinking -- is inherently good and pleasant for the virtuous, because determinacy belongs to the nature of the good.
notes: This passage begins the most metaphysically dense argument in IX.9, building toward the conclusion that friends are essential to happiness. -- The identification of life with the activity (energeia) of perception or thought connects to De Anima II.1-2, where Aristotle defines the soul as the first actuality of a natural body that has life potentially. -- The principle that the determinate (horismenon) is good while the indeterminate (aoriston) is bad echoes Pythagorean and Platonic metaphysics (cf. Plato's Philebus on limit and the unlimited). -- 'The nature of pain will become plainer in what follows' refers to the discussion of pleasure and pain in Book X.1-5.

refashioned:
But if life itself is good and pleasant -- which it seems to be, from the very fact that all people desire it, and especially those who are good and supremely happy, for whom life is most desirable and whose existence is the most supremely happy --

And if the person who sees perceives that he sees, and the person who hears perceives that he hears, and the person who walks perceives that he walks, and similarly for all other activities there is something that perceives that we are active, so that if we perceive, we perceive that we perceive, and if we think, we perceive that we think --

And if to perceive that we perceive or think is to perceive that we exist, since existence was defined as perceiving or thinking --

And if perceiving that one is alive is in itself one of the things that are pleasant, since life is by nature good and to perceive what is good present in oneself is pleasant --

And if life is desirable, and particularly so for good people, because to them existence is good and pleasant, since they are pleased by the consciousness of the presence in them of what is in itself good --

And if, as the virtuous person is to himself, so he is to his friend also, for his friend is another self --

Then, if all this is true, just as each person's own existence is desirable for him, so too, or nearly so, is the existence of his friend.
elegant:
Life is good and pleasant, especially for the virtuous. We are conscious not only of our activities but of our consciousness itself: perceiving that we perceive, thinking that we think. This self-awareness is awareness of our own existence, since to exist is to perceive and think. Consciousness of one's own goodness is inherently pleasant.

Since the virtuous person relates to his friend as to himself -- the friend being another self -- the friend's existence is desirable in the same way as one's own.
summary: Aristotle constructs a chain of reasoning: life is good and pleasant; we are aware of our own perceiving and thinking, which is awareness of our existence; this self-awareness is pleasant for the virtuous because they perceive the presence of genuine good in themselves. Since a friend is another self, the friend's existence is as desirable as one's own.
distilled: Since we find our own conscious existence desirable -- because self-awareness reveals the good within us -- and a friend is another self, our friend's existence is as desirable as our own.
notes: This passage contains one of the earliest philosophical analyses of self-consciousness (synaisthesis -- perceiving that one perceives). It anticipates Descartes' cogito by nearly two millennia, though the purpose is ethical rather than epistemological. -- The chain argument structure (protasis after protasis building to a single conclusion) is characteristic of Aristotle's formal demonstrations. -- The 'another self' (allos autos) doctrine reaches its fullest expression here, grounding the need for friends in the metaphysics of consciousness and existence. -- Modern phenomenologists (Husserl, Merleau-Ponty) have drawn on this passage in their analyses of intersubjectivity.

refashioned: Now his own existence was seen to be desirable because he perceived his own goodness, and such perception is pleasant in itself. He needs, therefore, to be conscious of the existence of his friend as well. And this will be realized in their living together and sharing in discussion and thought, for this is what living together would seem to mean in the case of human beings -- not, as with cattle, merely feeding in the same place.
elegant: Since one's own existence is desirable through perceiving one's own goodness, one needs to perceive the friend's existence too. This is achieved through shared life -- which for humans means sharing in discussion and thought, not merely occupying the same space like animals.
summary: Since we desire our own existence because we perceive our own goodness, we also need to perceive our friend's existence -- and this requires truly living together, which for humans means sharing in conversation and thought, not merely being physically near each other.
distilled: Human friendship is realized through shared thought and conversation -- for living together means sharing in rational activity, not merely occupying the same space.
notes: The contrast between human community (sharing in logos -- reason/speech/discussion) and animal herding echoes Politics I.2, where Aristotle argues that humans alone possess logos and therefore form genuine political communities. -- 'Discussion and thought' (logos and dianoia) emphasizes that friendship's shared life is fundamentally intellectual and communicative. -- This passage grounds the need for friends in the distinctively human mode of existence.

refashioned: If, then, existence is in itself desirable for the supremely happy person -- since it is by nature good and pleasant -- and if the existence of his friend is very much the same, then a friend will be one of the things that are desirable. Now whatever is desirable for the happy person he must have, or he will be deficient in that respect. The person who is to be happy will therefore need virtuous friends.
elegant: Since existence is desirable for the happy person and his friend's existence is equally desirable, a friend is among the things necessary for happiness. Without virtuous friends, the happy person would be deficient -- so happiness requires them.
summary: Since the happy person's own existence is desirable and his friend's existence is equally so, virtuous friends are among the things necessary for happiness. Without them, the happy person would lack something essential.
distilled: Virtuous friends are necessary for happiness because the friend's existence is as desirable as one's own, and to lack what is desirable is to be deficient.
notes: This passage completes the argument of IX.9 with a clean syllogistic conclusion. -- The claim that the happy person 'must have' what is desirable connects to the definition of happiness in Book I.7 as 'lacking in nothing' (mēdenos endees). -- This conclusion has been seen as one of Aristotle's most important departures from purely self-sufficient conceptions of the good life, such as those later developed by the Stoics and Epicureans.

refashioned:
Should we, then, make as many friends as possible? Or -- as with hospitality, where the advice "be neither a man of many guests nor one with none" is thought appropriate -- does the same apply to friendship? Should a person neither be friendless nor have an excessive number of friends?

To friends made with a view to utility, this saying seems thoroughly applicable, for doing services in return for many people is a laborious task and life is not long enough to accomplish it. Friends in excess of what is sufficient for our own life are superfluous and a hindrance to living nobly; we have no need of them.

Of friends made with a view to pleasure, a few are also enough -- like a little seasoning in food.
elegant: Should one have as many friends as possible? For friends of utility, the answer is clearly no: returning services to many is exhausting and life is too short. Excess utility-friends hinder noble living. Friends of pleasure also need be few -- like seasoning, a little suffices.
summary: One should not seek to maximize the number of friends. Friends of utility should be limited, since reciprocating services to many is burdensome and life is short. Friends of pleasure should also be few, like seasoning that requires only a small amount to be effective.
distilled: Friends of utility and pleasure should be few -- too many become a burden that hinders noble living.
notes: The hospitality maxim ('neither a man of many guests nor one with none') is from Hesiod, Works and Days 715. -- The seasoning metaphor is characteristically Aristotelian in its use of everyday analogies to illuminate ethical points. -- This passage opens IX.10 on the proper number of friends.

refashioned:
But as regards good friends -- friends of character -- should we have as many as possible? Or is there a limit to the number of one's friends, as there is to the size of a city? You cannot make a city of ten people, and if there are a hundred thousand, it is a city no longer. But the proper number is presumably not a single fixed number; rather, it is anything that falls between certain limits.

So too for friends: there is presumably a fixed range, perhaps the largest number with whom one can live together -- for that, we found, is thought to be the most essential feature of friendship. And one plainly cannot live with many people and divide oneself among them.

Furthermore, those friends must themselves be friends with one another if they are all to spend their days together, and it is difficult for this condition to be met with a large number. It is also hard to share intimately in the joys and griefs of many people, since it may well happen that one must rejoice with one friend and mourn with another at the same time.

Perhaps, then, it is best not to seek as many friends as possible, but as many as are enough for the purpose of living together. It would actually seem impossible to be a close friend to many people. This is why one cannot be in love with several people at once: love is ideally a sort of excess of friendship, and that can only be felt toward one person; therefore deep friendship too can only extend to a few.

This seems to be confirmed in practice, for we do not find many people who are friends in the deeply companionate way, and the famous friendships of this kind are always between two people. Those who have many friends and are intimate with all of them are thought to be genuine friends of no one -- except in the way appropriate to fellow citizens -- and such people are also called obsequious.

In the way appropriate to fellow citizens, it is indeed possible to be a friend of many and yet not be obsequious but genuinely good. But one cannot have with many people the friendship based on virtue and on who our friends truly are. We must be content if we find even a few such friends.
elegant:
There is a natural limit to character-based friends, just as there is to the size of a city. The limit is roughly the number one can truly live with -- since one cannot divide oneself among many. All the friends must also befriend one another, and it is hard to share intimately in many people's joys and sorrows simultaneously.

Deep friendship, like love, is an excess that can only be directed toward few. Famous friendships are always between two. Those intimate with everyone are truly close to no one. While civic friendship can extend widely, virtue-based friendship is rare -- we should be grateful to find even a few such friends.
summary: Character-based friendship has natural limits, like the size of a city. One can only truly live together with a few people, those friends must also befriend each other, and intimate sharing of joys and griefs cannot extend to many. Deep friendship, like love, can only be directed at a few. We should count ourselves fortunate to find even a small number of true friends.
distilled: Deep friendship, like love, can only extend to a few -- those intimate with everyone are truly close to no one, and we should count ourselves fortunate to find even a small number of genuine friends.
notes: The city-size analogy draws on Aristotle's discussion in Politics VII.4 (1326a-b), where he argues that a city must be large enough to be self-sufficient but small enough for citizens to know each other's characters. -- The claim that love (erōs) is 'an excess of friendship' interestingly subordinates romantic love to the broader category of friendship. -- The observation that famous friendships are between two people may allude to pairs like Achilles and Patroclus, Orestes and Pylades, or Damon and Pythias. -- The distinction between civic friendship (wide) and virtue friendship (narrow) maps onto the three types of friendship from Book VIII.3.

refashioned:
Do we need friends more in good fortune or in bad? They are sought in both situations. People in adversity need help, while those in prosperity need people to live with and to be the recipients of their generosity, for they wish to do well by others.

Friendship, then, is more necessary in bad fortune -- which is why useful friends are what one wants in that case. But friendship is more noble in good fortune, which is why we also seek good people as friends in prosperity, since it is more desirable to confer benefits on such people and to spend time with them.

For the very presence of friends is pleasant both in good fortune and in bad, since grief is lightened when friends share in our sorrow. This is why one might ask whether they literally share our burden, or whether -- without that actually happening -- their presence, by its pleasantness and by the thought that they grieve with us, makes our pain less. Whether grief is lightened for these reasons or for some other may be left aside; at any rate, what we have described does appear to occur.
elegant:
Friends are needed in both good and bad fortune, but differently. In adversity we need useful friends for help; in prosperity we need good friends to benefit and share life with. Friendship is more necessary in misfortune but more noble in good fortune.

Friends' presence is pleasant in both conditions. Grief is lightened when friends share our sorrow -- whether because they literally bear part of the burden or because their sympathetic presence makes pain more bearable.
summary: We need friends in both prosperity and adversity: useful friends for help in hard times, good friends to benefit and live with in good times. Friendship is more necessary in adversity but more noble in prosperity. The presence of friends lightens grief, whether through shared burden or sympathetic companionship.
distilled: Friendship is more necessary in misfortune but more noble in prosperity -- and in both conditions, friends' presence lightens our burdens and enriches our joys.
notes: The distinction between friendship being 'more necessary' in adversity and 'more noble' in prosperity reflects Aristotle's hierarchy of goods, where nobility (to kalon) ranks above necessity. -- The question of whether friends literally share our emotional burden or merely reduce it through pleasant presence is psychologically sophisticated and anticipates modern debates about empathy and emotional contagion in psychology.

refashioned:
But the presence of friends seems to involve a mixture of factors. The very sight of friends is pleasant, especially in adversity, and serves as a safeguard against grief -- for a friend tends to comfort us both by his presence and by his words, if he is tactful, since he knows our character and what pleases or pains us. But seeing a friend pained by our misfortunes is itself painful, for everyone avoids being a cause of pain to his friends.

For this reason, people of a manly nature guard against making their friends grieve with them. Unless a person is exceptionally insensible to pain, he cannot stand the distress that his own suffering causes his friends, and in general he does not welcome fellow-mourners because he is not himself given to mourning. But women and people of a womanly nature enjoy having sympathizers in their grief and love them as friends and companions in sorrow.

In all things, however, one should obviously take the better type of person as one's model.
elegant:
Friends' presence in adversity is a complex good. Seeing friends is pleasant and their tactful words comfort us, since they know our character. But seeing them pained by our misfortunes is itself painful, since we dislike causing friends distress.

People of strong character avoid burdening friends with their grief and reject excessive commiseration, while those of weaker temperament welcome sympathizers. In all things, one should model the stronger character.
summary: The comfort of friends in adversity is mixed with the pain of seeing them distressed on our behalf. People of strong character avoid burdening friends with their grief, while others welcome sympathizers. One should model the stronger approach, accepting comfort while avoiding being a cause of unnecessary pain to friends.
distilled: Strong character means accepting comfort from friends while guarding against burdening them with excessive grief -- valuing their wellbeing even in one's own suffering.
notes: The contrast between 'manly' and 'womanly' responses to grief reflects ancient Greek gender norms, where public emotional display was discouraged for men (cf. Pericles' Funeral Oration in Thucydides II.45). -- The psychological observation about the pain of causing friends to suffer is acute: it recognizes that empathy creates a feedback loop of distress. -- The emphasis on the 'tactful' (epidexios) friend who knows what to say connects to Aristotle's discussion of social virtues in Book IV.6-8.

refashioned:
On the other hand, the presence of friends in prosperity carries both a pleasant way of passing time and the pleasant awareness that our friends take pleasure in our good fortune. For this reason, it would seem that we ought to invite friends readily to share in our good fortune -- since a generous character is a noble one -- but summon them to our misfortunes only with hesitation. We ought to give them as little a share as possible in our troubles, hence the saying: "My own misfortune is enough."

We should call on friends most of all when they are able to help us greatly at little cost to themselves.
elegant: In prosperity, we should readily share our good fortune with friends, since generosity is noble. In adversity, we should hesitate to burden them, sharing as little of our troubles as possible. The best time to call on friends is when they can help us greatly at little cost to themselves.
summary: We should eagerly share our prosperity with friends but reluctantly impose our misfortunes on them. The ideal request for help comes when friends can do us great good at little cost to themselves.
distilled: Share your prosperity generously but your misfortune sparingly -- and call on friends most when they can help you greatly with little sacrifice.
notes: The saying 'enough is my misfortune' (arkei gar hē emē tychē) appears in various forms in Greek literature. -- The principle of minimizing the cost imposed on friends while maximizing benefit anticipates modern economic reasoning about efficient allocation of social capital. -- The characterization of generosity (to euergetikon) as noble connects to the virtue of liberality discussed in Book IV.1.

refashioned:
Conversely, it is fitting to go to the aid of those in adversity without being asked and without hesitation. For it is characteristic of a friend to render services, especially to those who are in need and have not demanded help; such action is nobler and more pleasant for both parties.

But when friends are prospering, we should join readily in their activities -- for they need friends for these purposes too -- while being slow to come forward as recipients of their generosity, for it is not noble to be eager to receive benefits. Still, we must be careful not to get the reputation of being joyless by pushing friends away; for that sometimes happens.

The presence of friends, then, seems to be desirable in all circumstances.
elegant:
Go to friends in adversity without being asked -- unsolicited help for those in need is nobler and more pleasant for everyone. In friends' prosperity, join their activities readily but be slow to accept their generosity, since eagerness to receive is not noble. Yet avoid repelling friends so firmly that you seem joyless.

Friends' presence is desirable in all circumstances.
summary: One should go to friends in adversity without waiting to be asked, since unsolicited help is nobler. In prosperity, join friends' activities but be reluctant to receive their generosity. Avoid both eagerness to receive and the coldness that repels others. Friends' presence is desirable in all circumstances.
distilled: Go to friends in adversity without being asked, join in their prosperity without seeking their generosity, and never be so self-sufficient that you repel the friends who wish to be present.
notes: The asymmetry -- rushing to help without being asked, but being slow to receive -- captures an important ethical insight about the relationship between giving and receiving. -- The warning against being a 'kill-joy' (aēdēs) shows Aristotle's awareness that excessive self-reliance can itself become a vice, connecting to his doctrine of the mean. -- This passage concludes the discussion of IX.11 on friendship in prosperity and adversity.

refashioned:
Does it not follow, then, that just as for lovers the sight of the beloved is what they love most -- and they prefer the sense of sight to the others because love depends on it most for its existence and its origin -- so for friends the most desirable thing is living together?

For friendship is a partnership, and as a person is to himself, so he is to his friend. Now in his own case, the consciousness of his own existence is desirable; therefore the consciousness of his friend's existence is desirable too. And the activity of this consciousness is produced when they live together; so it is natural that friends aim at this.

Whatever existence means for each kind of person -- whatever it is for the sake of which they value life -- that is what they wish to pursue together with their friends. And so some drink together, others play games together, others join in athletic exercises and hunting, and still others in the study of philosophy -- each group spending their days together in whatever they love most in life. For since they wish to live with their friends, they do and share in those things that give them the sense of living together.

Thus the friendship of bad people turns out to be an evil thing: because of their instability they unite in bad pursuits, and besides, they become evil by becoming like each other. But the friendship of good people is good, being strengthened by their companionship. And they are thought to become better too through their shared activities and by improving each other, for from each other they take the imprint of the qualities they admire -- hence the saying, "Noble deeds from noble men."

So much, then, for friendship. Our next task must be to discuss pleasure.
elegant:
As lovers prize the sight of the beloved above all, friends prize living together above all -- for friendship is a partnership, and the consciousness of a friend's existence is as desirable as consciousness of one's own.

Whatever people value most in life, they wish to share with friends: some drink, some play, some exercise, some philosophize together. The friendship of bad people becomes an evil, since they unite in bad pursuits and make each other worse. But the friendship of good people is strengthened by companionship -- they improve each other by modeling the qualities they admire. 'Noble deeds from noble men.'
summary: Friends, like lovers, prize togetherness above all -- since friendship is a partnership and consciousness of a friend's existence is as desirable as one's own. People share with friends whatever they value most in life. Bad people's friendships make them worse, but good people's friendships make them better, as they shape each other through the qualities they admire.
distilled: The essence of friendship is shared life -- and while bad friends make each other worse, good friends become better by taking the imprint of each other's virtues.
notes: This final passage of Book IX brings the discussion full circle, connecting the metaphysics of consciousness from IX.9 to the practical reality of shared daily life. -- 'Noble deeds from noble men' (esthlōn ... esthla) is attributed to Theognis (Elegies 35). -- The observation that friends shape each other's character -- for good or ill -- anticipates modern social psychology research on peer effects and behavioral contagion. -- The transition to pleasure (Book X) is significant: Books VIII-IX on friendship and Book X on pleasure together complete Aristotle's account of the components of the good life beyond virtue itself. -- The partnership (koinōnia) language echoes Politics I.1, where every community is established for some good.
